note: Mathlib/MeasureTheory/OuterMeasure/AE.lean:47 defines `ae` (the a.e./co-null filter) for any OuterMeasureClass; Mathlib/Probability/Notation.lean:63-67 defines `=ₐₛ`/`≤ₐₛ` ("almost surely") as this filter specialized to `volume`. Probability's "almost surely" is the case where μ is a probability measure, so the cited decl is strictly more general than the concept.
verify_note: decl exists; both cited line numbers checked byte-exact in the mathlib4 checkout (ae at AE.lean:47, =ₐₛ/≤ₐₛ at Notation.lean:63-67 defined as ae specialized to volume); generalization direction and hedged confidence are honest, no exact decl for "almost surely" exists to have been missed.

note: Mathlib/Analysis/Calculus/Taylor.lean:536 states f x - taylorWithinEval f n ... x = ∫ t in x₀..x, ((x-t)^n/n!) • iteratedDerivWithin (n+1) f ... t — Taylor's theorem with integral remainder. Cauchy's repeated-integration formula (n-fold antiderivative = 1/(n-1)! ∫(x-t)^{n-1} f(t) dt) is the special case with the lower Taylor terms vanishing; Mathlib only has the strictly more general Taylor statement, not the repeated-integration formula on its own.
verify_note: decl exists; cited statement matches Taylor.lean:536 verbatim; Cauchy's repeated-integration formula is genuinely recoverable as the special case of this theorem applied to the n-fold antiderivative g of f with g's lower Taylor coefficients at x0 vanishing — generalization relation and medium confidence are appropriately hedged, not overclaimed as exact.
